reviews.review_text:
Even after being a 10 year old game it still hold true coming from console to PC it definitely makes a huge difference I wish I would have gotten it on PC sooner but I was stuck in my console phase.

I would definitely recommend this to a friend who hasn’t played this game yet.

The store description doesn't do a very good job of explaining what this game actually is. It's an online, multiplayer-only title, where you'll spend most of your time playing alone in your own session. Unless you actively matchmake to play cooperatively with friends or strangers. The only places you will ever see other players are in the "social hubs", like safe houses and PvP-enabled areas. Otherwise, the social aspect of the game is pretty much useless.

As for the gameplay, it's a tactical looter-shooter. You roam a desolated yet beautiful New York City in winter, killing enemies and scavenging for better loot to optimize your loadout for different situations. Up until the post-game (when you finally hit level 30), the game heavily punishes you if you aren't tactical enough or if you are too conservative with your gear. It has a solid foundation, and it becomes much easier and far more fun once you reach the endgame.

Outside of combat, your goal is to rebuild a Base of Operations across three specialized wings. Each upgrade grants a new perk or ability. You'll have to gather resources to fund these upgrades, which are rewarded by completing campaign missions, side missions, and encounters scattered throughout manhattan.

The campaign itself is nothing stellar, it just works. However, the side content is not great at all. It is repetitive, tedious, and barely interesting especially when playing solo. Every single district features the exact same formula: three side missions and five encounters, and they all play out identically. You'll play the same mission several time, but at a different place, and it's not even well made.

The city itself looks fantastic, atmospheric, and nails the winter pandemic-apocalypse vibe. But, it quickly becomes monotonous as you notice how many assets and street layouts are simply copied and pasted. Ultimately, this is not really a great game. It can be fun enough if you don't take it too seriously, or if you have a group of friends to run through it with.
Game was fun at one point in time. Now you enter the dark zone and get killed instantly by other players. Story was good really enjoyed that. The PVP and scaling is horrible and was never reworked.

Used to play this game, loaded it up, character wasn't there. Then reloaded and character was there. Then I get on to play with a new buddy from work and my character is just totally gone. Haven't been more sad. 188 hours and now my ubisoft account says I've played 2 minutes. 

Ima cryu...

Strange enough I came from Division 2, most of the negative comments are about no support vs hackers (PVP mostly) and no updates or abandoned, though it is true due to Massive Focusing on the sequel and other version of the Division franchise more like (The Division Resurgence - Mobile), I do still recommend the game, content is much less than Division 2 but still lore and many other things to do PVE wise and still some activity in the Dark Zone (PVEP area).
I got the game for the lore and PVE content, while waiting on updates on the sequel (Division 2). 
FYI - Check the Ubisoft Connect App there might be discounts there as the Steam version is not really connected to the app offer wise.

What a game! The atmosphere is pretty much unmatched. Great game, even better with friends.
You got to play the Suvirival mode.
old game, still good. waiting to see when the server shuts

One of the most atmospheric and addictive looter-shooters ever made!

Even years after its release, Tom Clancy's The Division remains an absolute masterpiece in terms of world-building and atmosphere. The recreation of a winter, post-pandemic Manhattan is breathtaking. Walking through the snow-covered, desolate streets filled with abandoned cars, Christmas decorations, and dense fog creates a hauntingly beautiful and immersive vibe that no other game has managed to duplicate.

The gameplay loop is incredibly addictive. It perfectly blends tight, cover-based third-person shooter mechanics with deep RPG elements. Customizing your agent, unlocking skills by rebuilding the Base of Operations, and constantly hunting for better weapons and gear sets kept me hooked for dozens of hours. The combat is tactical and challenging, making every encounter with enemy factions feel intense.

And then, there is the Dark Zone. It is easily one of the most brilliant and nerve-wracking multiplayer concepts in gaming history. Entering this high-risk area to hunt for the best loot while constantly looking over your shoulder, never knowing if another player is going to help you or betray you at the extraction point, delivers a pure adrenaline rush.

Whether you play it completely solo or team up with friends in co-op, this game is an unforgettable tactical journey. A true gem!

This game has one of the best “empty city after everything went wrong” atmospheres I’ve ever seen. Snowy New York looks beautiful, cold, abandoned, and kind of depressing in the perfect way. You walk through the streets and it feels like the city is still alive, just very tired and covered in Christmas decorations that missed the apocalypse memo.

I really like the mix of cover shooting, loot, skills, and slow character progression. You clear missions, upgrade your base, improve your gear, and slowly turn your agent from “guy with a jacket and problems” into someone who can actually survive the city for more than five minutes.

Co-op is where it shines the most. Running missions with friends, healing each other, pushing through tough fights, and getting better loot has that classic “one more run” feeling. And then there’s the Dark Zone, which is basically a trust issue simulator with better jackets. You go in for loot, meet other players, and immediately start asking yourself if humanity was a mistake.

It’s stylish, atmospheric, tense, and still one of the coolest looter-shooter settings out there. The winter New York vibe alone makes it worth playing.
Loot and loot some more! Almost fun until you realize the enemies scale with your character...
Fun and enjoyable but the bullet sponge enemies drive me insane
This game was phenominal when it launched. There was nothing like it in gaming. The second game sucks compared to the first one.
Great game, bloody difficult on normal (for me anyway!) Opponents are very crafty and use tactics to attack such as flanking.

One of the most interesting games of the last Ten years. Still fun to play through even with the second game still popular. Most of my play time is on the ubisoft launcher but this is one of my favourite games.

Decent game but put off by UBISOFT Launcher.

The Ubisoft launcher will allow you to log in on the web but will tell you the same username/pw combo is false when using the launcher.

Step1: got to web and change password to existing password.

Step 2: mess about as Ubisoft require you to go back and forth with 2fa 3 times, despite not being able to recogbnize a correct password in the first place.

Step 3: verify yourself a few more times.

step4 : prepare to do this ♥♥♥♥ again next time you want to play.


Ubisoft launcher is awful and wastes time.

never play the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ survival dlc, its the biggest piece of ♥♥♥♥ and i hope whoever decided it was a good idea to add it got fired instantly and is stuck playing the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ survival eternally until he kills himself because thats the better option rather than playing that ♥♥♥♥ game mode

as much as i want to like this game, ubisoft's SERVER IS BAD!

A lot of the time I press an ability button, there's a delay when the game checks if you're not cheating, and more often than not, the ability gets cancelled, despite a full bar (and I retry to deploy, but it gets stuck in a cancel loop!)

Enemies won't die because of server validation, and if you and your co-op partners gang on 1 enemy, their server validation goes haywire, resulting in the boss having INFINITE HP and it will stay that way until all players leave the mission!

Immersive, gounded, and a pretty looking game. Love the hud deisgn. The premise of the game is very well set, grim dark, realistic and heoric. 

it does not force you to focus on the story, which I find a lack luster experience, the premise was not used to its full potential, the quest design is good but emphasis on the game's permise is poor. The game has a notion that you are changing the world around you, but it is not dynamic at all, things just respawn, the evnironment remains the same and you go back to farming.

This game needs to be played with friends, else it is not fun. The gear progression is also quite bad, it only ever picks up in power scaling after you finish the campaign after level 30. The end game loop is akin to all the looter shooters out there, power scale, run the same level over and over again, collect them all kind of a game. 

Worth playing until campaign finishes.

The game looses its initial immersive charm after about 20h, as the game is not dynamic things become stale and repitative rather quickly.
Absolutely fantastic world building and vibe.

Ubisoft account BS always. Can even make a claim on account because locked out for to many attempts to log in which I didn't attempt to log in, its simply clicking play in steam game library. Not the first time its happened Ubisoft = Ubilaim and soft
It's good I won't lie but its sad that this game doesn't get any events like the second game. Like the hardcore one or even other ones

Truly a one of a kind game. Timeless classic. Hard to believe the game is 10 years old now from past to present but the game is not perfect. Coming back/transitioning from console [PS4] and starting fresh on pc made me aware of the how bad the latencies on servers and hit registration was over the years of being not fully maintained from the moderation side of things so do keep that in mind when trying out the game. 

This game is much more grind heavy and RNG dependent than Division 2 (via targeted loot) but overall it has it's charms on top of it if you have the patience for it. Personally one of my all time favorite games to comeback to and relax on from it's immersion to it's dark and gritty environments. Worth a try if you have the patience to deal with it.

The division cames out 10 years ago , but nonetheless still the game of the genre !

But why is that ?

First of all , The context of the division 1

The game is based in Manhattan In 2015 after black friday , people started getting sick with an unknown illness spreaded by money. Thus making the society as we know , Collapse

You are a Sleeper agents of the government and your task is to put orders in all that mess.

The snowy and abandoned streets of Manhattan make the city feel completely lifeless, as if society disappeared overnight.

Unlike The Division 2, The Division is not the kind of game you can play with your brain turned off.

Every encounter feels dangerous, forcing the player to constantly adapt to enemy behavior and unpredictable situations. Different factions approach combat differently, making even simple fights feel tense and dynamic.

That constant pressure makes survival feel earned rather than guaranteed.

And because the game demands your full attention, every successful encounter becomes genuinely rewarding, creating a strong sense of accomplishment that keeps the experience addictive even years later.

Another reason why The Division still stands out today is the variety of experiences it offers without ever feeling disconnected from its world.

Each game mode completely changes the way you approach the game while still using the same character and progression system.

Underground turns Manhattan into a constantly changing maze filled with unpredictable encounters, while Survival transforms the city into a brutal snowstorm where enemies, temperature, limited resources, and isolation become just as dangerous as combat itself.

Because of this, the game constantly feels fresh and avoids the repetitive gameplay loop that affects many other looter shooters.

The Division achievements journey truly begins with the main story.

Manhattan slowly takes you by the hand and introduces you to everything the game has to offer. Every completed mission and every level gained gives a satisfying sense of progression, making your character feel stronger little by little.

But eventually, the Dark Zone starts calling you.

This dangerous area quickly becomes one of the most memorable parts of the game. Every encounter feels unpredictable, and trusting another player can easily become your last mistake. The constant tension and paranoia make the Dark Zone feel completely different from the rest of the game.

If you want to fully understand the world of The Division, you’ll also need to complete side missions and fully upgrade your base of operations.

And then come the collectibles.

Phone recordings, ECHO sequences, survival guides, missing agent reports… there are a huge amount of them. Collecting everything can sometimes become repetitive, but they add an incredible amount of immersion to the world.

Through these collectibles, you slowly discover how Manhattan collapsed, how civilians reacted to the virus, and how some people took advantage of the chaos for their own benefit. They make the city feel far more believable and human.

Once the main story is over, bringing a friend and replaying missions on harder difficulties becomes one of the best parts of the experience. Since the campaign itself is not extremely long, replaying it with tougher enemies creates a much more engaging challenge.

Then come the DLCs.

Underground is probably the weakest part of the game for me.

It fully embraces the core gameplay loop of The Division: shoot, loot, level up, repeat. At first, the constantly changing underground missions feel refreshing, but after spending dozens of hours repeating similar objectives, environments, and enemy encounters, the mode slowly becomes exhausting. At that point, loot becomes the main reason to continue playing.

Survival, however, is a completely different experience.

This DLC turns The Division into a brutal test of endurance. You constantly have to monitor your temperature, resources, equipment, positioning, and remaining time while surviving against enemies and the freezing blizzard surrounding Manhattan.

Every decision matters, and every mistake feels dangerous.

It is easily one of the most immersive and stressful experiences in the entire game. However, having to repeat long and difficult runs many times for achievements can eventually become frustrating despite how good the mode itself is.

Even after all these years, The Division still delivers an atmosphere and gameplay loop that very few looter shooters have managed to replicate.
Crouching/Hiding feature can be annoying at times. But, a cool game. If you like an Escape From New york vibe

I played nearly a thousand hours of this game on my Xbox. It was one of the first shooter games I ever played and by far it is the best. There is so much for you to grind for in this game. You could pull relentless all nighters on this game and still not be satisfied. The cherry on top to this is that the story is actually pretty good and very interesting. But besides that I completely recommend this game if you love to grind alone or even better with friends.

As someone with 500 hours in The Division 2,  I thought it would be cool to check this game out. Well the only thing I really appreciated was the visual presentation, NY in the winter is always a sight to behold and the devs really captured its beauty even in the ongoing apocalyptic setting. Movement and cover mechanics are alright, though it did take some time to figure out which objects serve as reliable cover. In terms of the story, I didnt get very far  but the opening is quite slow. What really frustrated me immediately is how bad the balancing feels for a solo player. In D2 I would try to do the main missions, on or 1 level below recommended because I like the challenge. In this game, however, I felt so penalized for it when I got  to the Joe Ferro fight. The main problem I felt with the combat was that the enemies not only possessed high health and armor pools but also dealt ridiculous burst damage, which is hard to counter as a solo player when even a single enemy targets you. The lack of a sheild or amor bar makes it even harder to notice burst dps. So although I made it all the way to the Joe Ferro fight on the mission underlevelled, I got my ass kicked repeatedly. Well, fair enough. So I leave the mission to grind EXP, mark an encounter nearby and head in that direction. A small group of enemies stand in my way so I start firing. I take out 2 of 3 and the last guy is a bounty who instantly deletes my hp. Alt+F4 and uninstall, never again.
Gorgeous game, still looks incredible in 2026. I originally beat Div 2 on Xbox and I am so glad I came back to play this one. 10/10 from me!

The Division is a game that I cant seem to forget, which is a good thing. After so many years of playing to late game then stopping for a long while, coming back and doing it again and again, it never ceases to amaze me how much this game gets correct.

Its one of the best ways ever to do an MMO style game. The entire game, all of it, can be played entirely solo if you want, and its segmented sections of open world play mixed with story based raids that are similar to dungeons in a standard fantasy setting, give this game such an approachable style of gameplay for almost all people while also feeling very gripping.  aside from the repeating voice lines over and over if you die often enough, the dialogue that will play between main characters while you are in a mission is actually pretty good, these people feel like they are written characters, they arnt perfect, but they arnt terrible either, they are written like people so you can relate to them on their level. The musical stings and over all soundtrack of this game is fantastic, it hits at the right moments and it has subtle and differing victory themes when you complete a raid that feels not only satisfying but provides this asthenic like you are playing through a film. Contrast this with so many MMO's out there and its stark how The Divisions presentation does kinda dwarf thoes other games when comparing it to a memorable experience. 

As you progress not only do you fee more used to your surroundings in the city but areas of the city become safer, more civilians show up and even greet you, even if its scripted, its one of the rare instances where in a video game, esp an MMO, it feels like im actually making a difference when I help the city while doing missions and removing threats.

The reason the dev team was able to accomplish this level of instancing is the game is separated between solo / co-op play and an entirely different section of the city that is devoted to PVEVP, called the Dark Zone. This makes the game a winning combo in my book as it allows you to enjoy the game you bought while not being forced into PVP or even to group up at all, if you want to. but its there to engage with if you are interested. 

Over all the game, to me, is a gem and will stand the test of time in its environmental story telling, interesting and believable characters, fantastic sound track and solid game play that keeps me coming back year after year.

The setting is great, gameplay is fun, a lot of bang for your buck
(i bought it when it was 3 euros)
Played the heck out of this on ye olden Playstation many years ago, so  27 hours like 270 in total

I played this game back in 2016 when it released. Reached level 30 and get to endgame content. Stopped playing because pvp and extractions weren't my vibe then. 

I returned 10 years later and I have to say, this is one of my favorite games. Replaying through it with my partner before we head into The Division 2. For a 10 year old game it holds up. (PvP and extractions are now my vibe)

(I have most of my hours on uplay, about 150, so I did do a lot of things but not everything.)
+ Very detailed open world. Looks very good.
+ Base Building.
+ Weapons.
+ Gameplay. This is by far the biggest plus. Very fun gameplay.
+ Dark Zone. Not as fun nowadays but back when this game came out this was almost perfect.
+ Story. Even though the corona main story is not really that good, I really liked being the second wave of Division and finding out what happened in this city the further I got into the game. The collectibles fit in well into the world but I would be lying if I said I liked collecting them all, cause there are just too many of them.

- Side missions and open world activities are always exactly the same.
- Mute main protagonist.
- Map could be better.
- Blood looks like paint.
- Some server issues can cause enemies to go down with a delay which can be annoying.

I'd played Division 2 before this and loved it. Its my favorite loot n' shoot outside of the Borderlands games, and on top of that its rendition of D.C. (a place Ive visited many times) is spectacular and fun to explore, and the Settlement system is a really compelling structure for progression.

Division 1, by comparison, is boring as sin. Its structure is "here's a bunch of basic combat encounters in random places" + "here's some more hubs where you can find more basic combat encounters in random places". With NY being a much more monotonous setting to navigate and having significantly less reason to explore, the loop of Division 1 is a whole lot of running followed by a combat encounter or two. There are no grand story missions with extensive and memorable levels in the two hours Ive played, which you get regularly in Division 2, even within the first couple hours.

Especially now that Division 2 has been getting content updates for a decade, don't bother spending any amount of money on this game. I regret spending even the dirt cheap $5 I paid during a steep sale.

Bit too heavy on the "MC is our hero and savior" trope, story feel rushed and very lacking. But, we came for a shooter to be fair. The map does feel bigger than it is, detailed world, surface-level but interesting NPCs. Overall, why not give it a shot. Also let me pet the dogs, PLEASE let me pet the dogs.
Take off your Critical Thinking Cap, and it's a decent looter shooter with a friend

A classic Ubisoft game: you play a character in a world where there's something going on and they try their best to immerse you in it. The immersion succeeds well: the atmopshere, hidden voice logs, missions all add to the feeling.

I have to give it a thumbs down however because people barely play it anymore, great if you have a buddy to play with but otherwise don't assume you will run into other players.

Also, misophonia warning: lots of coughing.
Genuinely some of the worst gunplay and movement controls I have ever seen in a game.
Gets stale quick. The gunplay isn't good enough to justify the gameplay loop. I'd recommend Destiny 2 over this, but the game is dead.
Like the story of it, immersive, and being able to play with my husband and son.
There's nothing funnier than playing a single-player campaign with lag 🤡

Was going to play for nostalgic purposes.
it asked to make a ubisoft account.
"uninstall"
Game wouldn't even work for me and Steam was so cheap they wouldn't refund me with a total of 25 minutes played. Don't get scammed by Steam on this one.
I played this a lot on the Xbox One. It's a great game. It's just sad to see it's not popular anymore.
Decent game. Looter-shooter. Worth giving it a try.
New York is full of bad guys but the real enemy is my backpack being full every 5 minutes

[h1] THE BEAUTIFUL, DESOLATE WINTER OF MANHATTAN [/h1]

[b][i]  Surviving the virus outbreak in a snow-covered, ruined New York City during Christmas is arguably one of the greatest atmospheric achievements in gaming.

Running through the tense, brutal streets of the Dark Zone, perfecting the looter-shooter build grinds, and mastering tactical team plays keeps you hooked for countless sessions. Ubisoft created a masterpiece of world design here that still holds up perfectly.

When society falls, we rise. 9/10  [/i][/b]

What an absolute slog. 40 hours to get through most of the story and side missions and hunt down a bunch of the collectibles. The movement doesn't feel great, and the map is frustratingly closed off. It's also incredibly repetitive, with very little new information or plot development across the story missions, some of which I'd sworn I'd done before because the environment and objectives were so similar.
models looks like the product of multigenerational incest

Weird interface with unusual control settings make it difficult to become accustomed to it. No ability to crouch other than by hiding behind something, which is then difficult to get away from - unlike any other games. Video is indistinct and lacks contrast and clarity - lots of flaky bits too. Difficult to play the game other than by running around wildly firing at things - the layout means no strategical element is possible.

The Division actually has great PvP mechanics, which makes the cheating situation even more disappointing.

The Dark Zone can be incredibly fun when fights are legit. The combat feels good, the tension is real, and the gameplay has a lot of potential. The problem is Ubisoft barely seemed to even try stopping the cheating. Hackers and exploit abusers were everywhere, and it constantly ruined the experience for legitimate players.

It’s frustrating because underneath all the cheating is a genuinely good PvP game. But when the developers don’t properly deal with the problem, it kills the competitive side of the game fast.

Amazing PvP potential.
Terrible anti-cheat support.
grate game ubisoft connet sucks
This game is AMAZING for it's time. Ubisoft at it's BEST.
